text.title: Domain Interactions, Assessment Procedure, and Gotchas
text.summary: Domain interaction chains, health assessment procedure, common mistakes, and agent-specific gotchas for PMBOK 7 performance domain audits.

section.title: Domain Interaction Chains
section.p: When one domain degrades, specific other domains are predictably affected. Use these chains to identify root causes and prioritize remediation.

list.item: Stakeholder struggles → Team (morale), Delivery (unclear acceptance), Measurement (disputed metrics)
Team struggles → Project Work (slower execution), Delivery (quality drops)
Development Approach struggles → Planning (wrong artifacts), Project Work (wrong cadence), Delivery (wrong increments)
Planning struggles → Project Work (unclear priorities), Delivery (missed scope), Measurement (no baseline)
Measurement struggles → all domains (blind decisions without data)
Uncertainty struggles → all domains (risk materializes everywhere simultaneously)

rule.statement: When multiple domains are red, identify the single domain whose improvement unblocks the most others; fix that domain first before attempting parallel remediation.
rule.rationale: Parallel remediation across multiple Red domains spreads PM attention too thin; sequenced priority produces completed fixes faster than simultaneous partial progress.

list.item: Gather artifacts: retros, status reports, risk register, sprint logs, issue tracker exports
Declare the evidence time window (e.g., "last 30 days"); assessments without a declared window are not reproducible
Score each domain with one quoted evidence item per RAG color
Identify the weakest domain and explain its cross-domain impact
Publish no more than 3 priority actions; more than 3 cannot be worked in parallel
Track domain health as a trend (direction matters more than absolute level)

rule.statement: Run the assessment on a fixed monthly cadence AND on triggers: incident, scope change, leadership change, team-composition change.
rule.rationale: Monthly cadence catches gradual drift; trigger-based re-scoring catches sudden regressions that monthly timing misses.

antipattern.description: Treating domains as sequential rather than concurrent.
antipattern.reason: All eight domains require attention simultaneously; skipping one because "we're in planning now" leaves it unmonitored until a late-stage crisis surfaces.

antipattern.description: Process over outcomes — tracking activities within a domain rather than domain outcomes.
antipattern.reason: A team can run all the right activities (risk workshops, stakeholder meetings) while domain outcomes (threats mitigated, stakeholders engaged) deteriorate.

antipattern.description: One-time assessment without repeat on a fixed cadence.
antipattern.reason: Domain health changes; a one-time Green assessment gives false confidence for months while conditions drift to Red.

antipattern.description: Agent over-rates Team and Stakeholder domains Green because retros are polite.
antipattern.reason: Counterweight with attrition data and NPS/satisfaction scores; retro politeness is not a valid evidence source for health.

antipattern.description: Agent scores AND prescribes actions in one pass.
antipattern.reason: Bias compounds; split into two prompts: first scoring with evidence, then separate prescriptions.

antipattern.description: Agent produces assessments without declaring the evidence time window.
antipattern.reason: Undated assessments cannot be reproduced or audited; require explicit time window in every assessment output.

text.reference: Emits a fresh eight-row assessment skeleton with weakest-domain section
